nativeRange.habitat: Found on coral and rocky reefs across the tropical and subtropical Atlantic, the Big Eye Black Bar Soldierfish spends daylight hours sheltering in caves, ledges, and overhangs, then emerges after dark to forage in open water.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical to subtropical Atlantic reef, typically 2–35 m depth

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs

diet.notes: Generally advised to feed a varied meaty diet — mysis, enriched brine, chopped marine shrimp/fish, and frozen carnivore preparations — once or twice daily. Has been reported to feed mainly on planktonic crustaceans in the wild. New arrivals often need live saltwater feeder shrimp to break onto prepared foods; feeding shortly after lights-out tends to elicit a stronger response.

avoidWith: small dither fish (chromis, small gobies) that may be eaten

imageCandidates.notes: NOAA/FGBNMS/Hickerson, 3200x2400. Sharp in-situ group of five Blackbar Soldierfish showing the diagnostic dark bar behind the gill cover, big eyes, reddish body and white fin edges; best represents this shoaling species.
NOAA Photo Library, 2560x1920. Crisp single specimen, whole fish in frame, clear dark gill bar and large eye against reef.
David J. Stang, 1000x665. Vibrant red lateral view, whole fish, dark bar and big eye visible; lower resolution than others.

summary: Hardy nocturnal reef predator with oversized eyes and a distinctive dark bar behind the gill cover. Generally peaceful toward similar-sized tankmates but tends to eat small fish and shrimp.
careNotes:
Strictly nocturnal: spends daylight hours tucked in caves and overhangs, emerging to forage after the tank lights go off. A dimly lit aquarium with extensive rockwork containing multiple cave-sized refuges is generally advised. Despite the 'peaceful' label commonly applied by retailers, Myripristis jacobus is an opportunistic predator and has been reported to ingest small fish, ornamental shrimp, and other small crustaceans, so tankmates should be chosen with adult mouth-gape in mind. Conspecifics are best introduced simultaneously as small groups in tanks large enough to give each individual its own retreat; otherwise intraspecific aggression has been observed. Net-handling should be minimised — the species' large, delicate eyes are easily abraded by netting. Most specimens transition onto frozen and prepared meaty foods after an initial period of live-feeder offerings; target-feeding near lights-out tends to be the most reliable way to break new arrivals. Reef-safe with caution: corals are generally ignored, but motile ornamental invertebrates are at risk.

The 'Big Eye Black Bar Soldierfish' trade name and 'Blackbar Soldierfish' common name refer to the same species (Myripristis jacobus); no distinct colour morphs are recognised in the aquarium trade.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented for the aquarium trade; the species is a pelagic spawner with planktonic larvae, which is generally incompatible with standard home-aquarium rearing.

additional.notes: Cross-checked tank-size (600 L recommended), predation risk to small fish/crustaceans, and net-handling warning. Tank-size spread: LiveAquaria 380 L vs Reefapp 600 L — biased larger and rounded to 500 L per playbook rule for an active nocturnal predator reaching ~25 cm.

fish.breedingNotes: The species is a pelagic spawner, releasing eggs and sperm into open water where larvae develop as part of the plankton — a strategy that makes home-aquarium breeding exceptionally difficult, and no documented captive-breeding success has been reported.
